summary: Gruber reviews the 2021 iPad Mini, highlighting its remarkable value proposition: an A15-equipped tablet that rivals M1 Mac single-threaded performance starting at $500. He frames it as effectively an 'iPad Air Mini,' noting it matches or exceeds the current iPad Air in most specs while costing $100 less. His main complaints are the lack of Face ID (inferior to his iPad Pro experience when using a keyboard), the 64GB base storage, and certain iPadOS interface elements that feel poorly optimized for the Mini's smaller screen. Overall, he finds it a terrific device, especially praising the thumb-typing experience and Center Stage camera.
key_points: The iPad Mini with A15 chip offers single-threaded CPU performance comparable to an M1 Mac for just $500, representing tremendous value
It's effectively an 'iPad Air Mini' — matching the Air's design language with round-cornered display, Touch ID on power button, and USB-C, while pulling ahead with A15, 5G, and Center Stage camera
Touch ID without Face ID is a significant ergonomic downgrade when using the iPad with an external keyboard, and Gruber argues iPadOS should support Apple Watch unlocking like macOS does
iPadOS has UI scaling inconsistencies on the Mini — the Dock accepts 16 tiny icons but the Command-Tab switcher only shows 5 apps, suggesting the OS isn't fully optimized for this screen size
The Mini's size makes it uniquely good for thumb-typing with the on-screen keyboard, including split keyboard support that larger iPad Pros inexplicably lack
money_quotes: For $500, you can buy an iPad that's more or less as fast, if not faster, at single-threaded CPU performance as an M1 Mac.
It is, effectively, an iPad Air Mini.
I'm not sure if you've heard, but video calls have become a big deal over the last two years.
That the iPad Mini offers several technical advantages over last year's iPad Air is pure gravy.
Having to reach over and touch the Touch ID button on the iPad is, simply, an inferior experience compared to Face ID.
I think it'd be very cool if iPadOS supported unlocking via Apple Watch, much like MacOS does.
There are just certain visual elements in iPadOS that feel inconsiderately shrunken on the iPad Mini, in a way that is not true for iOS on an iPhone Mini.
If you like the idea of using an iPad as a sort of big-ass iPhone, the iPad Mini is obviously the iPad for you.
key_insight: The 2021 iPad Mini delivers iPad Air-class design and M1-rivaling performance at a lower price, making it a remarkable value, though iPadOS still isn't fully optimized for its smaller screen.
